content: When I enter a new team of people, this is especially common being in the army. I'll generally not try and get other people to—I won't try and influence other people's opinions about me. I generally just go to work, and instead of telling people how they should view me or how I wish to be viewed, I simply get to work, and through my actions allow people to form opinions about me. This generally I find, especially with the way leadership comes in, and I'm going to do this, I'm going to do that. I'm going to make changes to the way your day-to-day life goes in these ways. I find that that's a little bit condescending, and people generally don't respond to that. They hold people up against that as a kind of expectation, and then judge people against the combination of how they feel and how and what they promised. Instead, if you just go to work and people see the changes that you are able to make or the benefits in their training, their day-to-day, they will generally form better opinions of you. I think this generally is because people don't like being told what to think of people, and people don't enjoy—especially soldiers—don't enjoy the normal condescending leadership. I'm your new lieutenant. I'm going to make all these good changes when they know that nothing is going to happen. Whereas if you actually make things happen for them, they know how hard that is, especially positive things, so they will respond very positively to that.
